rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LEARTEASE ELIZABETH WILSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Extraordinary Fees And Reimbursement Of Costs
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/29/25. Prior to a full review of the petition, the following issues must be addressed:  The petition does not contain a prayer. Please file an amended petition. Notice of Hearing on the filing of the amended petition will also be needed.  Timesheets have not been filed for ordinary services, which are needed to determine whether stautory fees are sufficient to compensate for all services performed.  Please see Local Rule 14.57 for costs that are not reimbursable and revise the costs schedule accordingly.  Statement of facts is needed addressing California Rules of Court 7.702. Please be sure to include the amount requested for each category of services performed (including for time spent on, and in support of, each petition that was filed).  Upon a cursory review of the timesheets for extraordinary services, it appears that there are statutory services listed. The entries must show how the services are extraordinary in nature.  Totals (time and fees) were not provided on the timesheets.  Proposed order has not been filed. All documents to be filed by 7/14/25.

rulings.Calendar Matter: REPORT OF STATUS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. Issues posted for the hearing on 5/27/25, have not been addressed, so the hearing would ordinarily have been continued again; however, the executor requests in his very succinct petition filed on 3/28/25 (which does not meet the requirements of LRSF 14.35 J.) sixty days to file his accounting, which time has already passed. Furthermore, pecuniary bequests have been accruing interest (7%) since 8/10/22, including the $100,000 gift to the executor, which does not appear to be fair to the residuary beneficiaries (charities). [Probate Code Sections 12001 and 12003.] Please have the CASE NUMBER and the CASE NAME ready for the clerk. Parties appearing must check in for the hearing using their FULL NAME. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LUCRECIA FONTES REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 04/23/2015 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Relief From Courts February 20, 2025 Order; And For Leave To File Objection To First Amended Petition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Feliciana Medina's Motion for Relief from Court's February 20, 2025 Order, and for Leave to File Objection to First Amended Petition ("Motion"). The Motion is DENIED, for the following reasons. Background: On September 9, 2024, Kisha Palmer, as Trustee of the Lucrecia Fontes Revocable Trust, dated April 23, 2015, filed Petition for Instructions and Order: 1. Determining Effect of Interlineations; 2. Determining Current Vested Beneficiaries; 3. Approving and Authorizing Trust Distributions; 4. Approving Actions of Trustee and Settling Account. Movant is listed on the September 13, 2024 Notice of Hearing for the initial hearing set on October 29, 2024. The hearing was continued to December 31, 2024, then January 28, 2025, then February 18, 2025, on request of Petitioner's counsel. On January 10, 2025, Petitioner filed First Amended Petition for Instructions and Order: 1. Determining Effect of Interlineations; 2. Determining Current Vested Beneficiaries; 3. Approving and Authorizing Trust Distribution; 4. Approving Actions of Trustee and Settling Account. Movant is listed on the January 10, 2025 Notice of Hearing for the February 18, 2025 hearing. Per the Minutes of the February 18, 2025 hearing, Justin Hedemark appeared for Petitioner Kisha Palmer via Zoom. No other party appeared. The court acknowledged a potential venue issue, and decided to accept jurisdiction under Code of Civil Procedure section 392(a)(1). Per the Minutes, "Mr. Hedemark confirmed that no objections or communications from any interested parties had been received as of the hearing date. The court accepted the finding that the settlor's handwritten interlineations made in 2018 and 2019 were valid amendments to the trust. The court took the petition under submission with the intent to grant the petition as prayed." On February 20, 2025, Order: 1) Determining Effect of Interlineations; 2) Determining Current Vested Beneficiaries; 3) Approving and Authorizing Trust Distributions; and 4) Approving Actions of Trustee and Settling Account was entered. Movant received a copy of the Order from Petitioner's attorney on March 25, 2025, as an attachment to an email (Medina Decl., par. 14). The instant Motion was filed May 30, 2025. Discussion: The party seeking relief under Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b) has the burden of proving one of the statutory grounds for relief by a preponderance of the evidence. (Hopkins & Carley v Gens (2011) 200 CA4th 1401, 1410; Kendall v Barker (1988) 197 CA3d 619, 623-624.) A judge may grant discretionary relief under section 473(b) when the movant shows a material mistake of fact or law that caused them to fail to file a timely response to the pleadings. (See, e.g., Lieberman v Aetna Ins. Co. (1967) 249 CA2d 515, 523-524, defendant mistakenly thought only codefendant had been served.) The movant bears the burden of proof, and must identify the mistake that led to the failure to file a timely response and may not merely assert a general state of misapprehension or ignorance. (Hopkins & Carley v Gens (2011) 200 CA4th 1401, 1410-1411.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LUCRECIA FONTES REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 04/23/2015 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Relief From Court'S February 20, 2025, Order, And For Leave To File Objection To Firstvamended Petition (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] Further, Movant's characterization of the February 18, 2025 hearing as the first and only time Petitioner sought an order finding her to be the sole beneficiary of decedent's home is incorrect. The Medina Declaration at paragraph 19 states, "[T]he one time I made a mistake, the trustee argued that I should be removed as a beneficiary of the assets from the sale of the home." The initial Petition and First Amended Petition expressly stated what Petitioner believed the interlineations provided, and how that would affect Movant and the other individuals listed in the original Trust instrument. The Petition and the First Amended Petition both facially sought in the prayer an order that would, among other things, find the interlineations on the Trust attached to the Petition - which included Movant's name crossed out in certain categories of bequests - were valid amendments and that Petitioner alone was entitled to the residue of the Trust (See, Pet. at 18:14-28 and First Amended Pet. at 22:18-23:10). Under the heading, "The Trust was Revocable when Lucrecia Amended It and the Interlineations Constitute Valid Amendments to the Trust that Should be Honored by the Court," both the initial Petition and the First Amended Petition argue, In this case, Lucrecia changed certain addresses and the distributive sections of her Trust as detailed in Article V. Beneficiaries on pages 2-3. Specifically, Lucrecia's handwritten notes cross out beneficiaries of personal property, remainder beneficiaries, and addresses for certain people referenced in the Trust. These handwritten interlineations include her initials "LF" followed by the dates 2/23/18 and 2/2/19 respectively, which a reasonable person can conclude stand for the February 23, 2018 and February 2, 2019. These dates coincide with a time period that Lucrecia was still the acting Trustee. Accordingly, Lucrecia's handwritten interlineations along [with] initials and the dates constitute "a writing other than a will, signed by the settlorand delivered to the trustee." (Cal. Prob. Code [sec]15401.) Therefore, Lucrecia's Trust was appropriately amended by her during her lifetime through the handwritten interlineations and must be treated as effectively changing the distributive pattern of trust assets and the beneficiaries entitled to personal property and cash. Pet. at 9:7-18; FAP at page 12:11-22. In arguing the matter's similarity to case law, both the initial Petition and the First Amended Petition argue, "Lucrecia's interlineations and changes to the Trust affect only certain named individuals addresses, beneficiaries, and their distributive share or percentage of the overall Trust property. The sections in question are Article IV.(A) Trustees on page 1; Article V.(B)(1), (2), and (3) Distributions on page 3. None of the amendments purport to change legal provisions, the powers of the trustee, or otherwise rewrite large portions of the Trust. They simply and clearly strike out addresses, individual names, and include the testator's initials and date next to the initials." (Pet. at 11:1-7; FAP at 14:3-9.) Both the initial Petition and the First Amended Petition go on to argue, under the heading "Lucrecia Effectively Amended the Distribution Pattern in Her Trust," "Pursuant to Lucrecia's handwritten interlineations, she changed the distributive sections of her Trust as detailed in Article V. Beneficiaries on pages 2-3. Specifically, Lucrecia's handwritten notes cross out beneficiaries of personal property as well as remainder beneficiaries." (Pet. at 11:22-25; FAP at 14:24-27.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LUCRECIA FONTES REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 04/23/2015 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Relief From Court'S February 20, 2025, Order, And For Leave To File Objection To First Amended Petition (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] Movant's failure to respond to the Petition was affirmative and intentional, although potentially misinformed. The court is not required to, and will not, grant relief under section 473(b) due to Movant's failure to exercise timely diligence and ascertain what the law required of her in the face of a Petition that directly threatened her expected inheritance. (McClain v. Kissler (2019) 39 Cal. App. 5th 399, 405, 424.) A self-represented person's ignorance of the law or legal procedure, or failure to properly represent her interests cannot constitute excusable mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect as those terms are used in section 473(b). (Hopkins & Carley v. Gens (2011) 200 Cal. App. 4th 1401, 1412-13, "[R]elief may be properly denied where the record shows only 'ignorance of the law coupled with negligence in ascertaining it.'") As stated in Hopkins & Carley v. Gens (2011) 200 Cal. App. 4th 1401, 1413-14, The law does not entitle a party to proceed experimentally without counsel and then turn back the clock if the experiment yields an adverse result. [ ]Rather, "when a litigant accepts the risks of proceeding without counsel, he or she is stuck with the outcome, and has no greater opportunity to cast off an unfavorable judgment than he or she would if represented by counsel." [citations.] The "naïveté" of lay litigants in "rely[ing] on themselves to protect their substantial legal interests" does not afford a ground for relief from adverse results. Rappleyea v. Campbell (1994) 8 Cal.4th 975, 979. Trustee shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LUCRECIA FONTES REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 04/23/2015 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For Relief From Court'S February 20, 2025, Order, And For Leave To File Objection To Firstvamended Petition; Declaration Of Rafael Godoy; Declaration Of Feliciana Medina
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Here, the court agrees with Movant that her miscalendaring of the operative hearing would qualify as an excusable mistake of fact under the meaning of section 473(b). A mistake is excusable when a reasonably prudent person under the same or similar circumstances might have made the same error. (McClain v Kissler (2019) 39 CA5th 399, 414-415.) However, as evidenced by Movant's supporting declaration, excerpted below, the calendaring mistake is not what caused Movant to fail to timely raise an opposition to the Petition. Based on her own declaration, Movant was not aware of the necessity of timely lodging an objection to the Petition, even if she had successfully appeared at the February 18, 2025 hearing. At Medina Decl. par. 3, "Based on representations made by Mr. Hedemark in a letter dated July 26, 2023, I thought it was clear that I would be a beneficiary of my aunt's real and personal property as it was typed out in the original trust. Mr. Hedemark's letter stated that the trust was now irrevocable and that the terms could "not be amended or changed." I took this to mean that I would be an heir and received distributions from the sale of my aunt's house and personal property." At Medina Decl. par. 4 "I hired lawyer Magany Abbas to help me understand the trust and the documents sent to me from Mr. Hedemark. Ms. Abbas told me, that under the original terms of the trust I was the beneficiary of my aunt's trust, including assets from the sale of the real and personal property. Ms. Abbas helped me set up my own estate plan including a trust to receive the assets I was going to inherit from my aunt." At Medina Decl. par. 15 "At the time I read the letter and Order [from Hedemark, sent via email on March 25, 2025], I did not know the difference between personal property and real property." At Medina Decl. par. 17 "I was not sure of what the Order [Hedemark] sent me meant and I arranged to see another attorney. I then met with Rafael Godoy." At Medina Decl. par. 18, "Mr. Godoy explained the Order to me." At Medina Decl. par. 20 "Mr. Hedemark never informed me that the court had ruled against my interest in regard to the real property." Medina states in her declaration at paragraph 21, "If would [sic] have been at the hearing I would have objected to the proposed interpretation of the scratch marks over my name and asked for time to get my own attorney." Movant's statement, however, is contradicted by her own declaration. Until she spoke with attorney Godoy and he explained the legal impact of the February 20, 2025 Order, Movant assumed she would inherit half of all of her aunt's trust estate, wrote her own estate plan on that assumption, and had no awareness of the Petition's pending impact on her expected inheritance. While that expectation appears to have been based on the advice of counsel, there is no attorney declaration of culpability to consider, and, importantly, that is not offered as the grounds on which to grant relief under section 473(b).

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LUCRECIA FONTES REVOCABLE TRUST DATED 04/23/2015 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Relief From Court'S February 20, 2025, Order, And For Leave To File Objection To First Amended Petition (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] The next two pages of both the initial Petition and the First Amended Petition are Petitioner's recreation of the handwritten interlineations of Article V. Beneficiaries, Sections (B)(1), (2) and (3) of the Trust instrument, specifically the cross-outs of Movant's name and address, and two other individuals' names and addresses, throughout the Section. Relevant to this Motion, Section (B)(3) states, "[T]he Trustmaker gives the residue (25% each) of her trust estate to the following beneficiaries or their issue by right of representation: [.]" In the Petition's recreation of the interlineations, three of the four names and addresses listed as beneficiaries in Section (B)(3) are crossed out, including Movant's name and address. In both the initial Petition and the First Amended Petition, this section concludes with the summary of Petitioner's requested distribution pattern, based on her reading of the interlineations' legal significance and effect: 1. The ultimate effect of these amendments result in the following distributive pattern: 1. All of the trustmaker's jewelry, clothing, household furniture and furnishings, personal automobiles, and other tangible articles of a personal nature, or her interest in such property, not otherwise specifically disposed of, together with any insurance on said property shall be divided and distributed equally to: KISHA PALMER, and FELICIANA ANDREA MEDINA. 2. No specific bequests are made; and 3. The Trustmaker gives the residue of her estate to KISHA PALMER "ONLY". Accordingly, Petitioner prays for an Order from this Court approving the above-referenced distribution schedule and authorizing her to make distributions in accordance with the Testator's wishes and outlined herein. Pet. at 13:8-19; FAP at 16:11-22. Further, correspondence between attorney Magany Abbass and Petitioner's counsel in March of 2024, months before the initial Petition was filed, shows Abbass as Medina's attorney asking Petitioner's counsel whether there would be a Heggstad petition filed regarding the interlineations on the Trust document (Exhibit B to Hedemark Declaration). Movant states she contacted attorney Magany Abbass again after receiving a copy of the initial Petition and Notice of Hearing in September 2024, that Abbass emailed Hedemark seeking information about the Petition, and received no reply (Medina Decl. par 14). Movant does not discuss whether she otherwise sought to understand what Petitioner was asking from the court prior to the February 18, 2025 hearing. Despite initially bringing the interlineated Trust and then the initial Petition to Ms. Abbass, it appears Medina otherwise proceeded self-represented in the matter, until this Motion was prepared and filed. A proposed Objection to First Amended Petition is attached to attorney Godoy's declaration in support of the Motion; in two pages, it presents an alternative interpretation of the interlineations, objects to the First Amended Petition's allegations of when, why, and by whom the interlineations were made, and asks the court to find Medina takes a fifty-percent interest in the Trust residue. There is no discussion as to why Movant did not raise any of these objections - or any objections at all - during the months between the initial Petition's filing and the February 18, 2025 hearing.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF THOMAS H CHIDLEY
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Appearance required for failure to address Examiner's Notes emailed on 3/9/25 to petitioner (at chidleys@yahoo.com). The issues are as follows: · The will was not witnessed. · DE-147S has not been filed (which must be attached to a cover sheet, indicating that it is a confidential filing). · Proof of Publication has not been filed. · Proposed order has not been filed (which must be completed with the assumption that the petition will be granted as requested). · The caption of the petition does not match the body of the petition. Please confirm whether Petitioner is requesting full authority under the Independent Administration of Estate of Act. · Petition requests her appointment as executor; however, there is no one nominated under the will. Remote appearances may be made. Please review Local Rule 14.1. The following information pertains to appearances by Zoom: WHEN ENTERING "YOUR NAME" ON ZOOM, BOTH THE FIRST AND LAST NAME, AS WELL THE CASE NUMBER, MUST BE INCLUDED. Meeting ID: 160 225 4765 Password: 514879 To appear by telephone through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252, and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE TRUST A UNDER THE WILL OF EDWARD GRANDI, ALSO KNOWN AS ED GRANDI, DECEASED
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Appointment Of Successor Trustee
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 9/10/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) A copy of the order establishing the testamentary trust must be filed. (2) Copies of other documents referenced in the petition (order appointing another trustee, proof of the death of that successor trustee, the successor trustee's appointment of a co-trustee) must also be filed. (3) All petitions involving a trust must state the names and last known addresses of all vested or contingent beneficiaries. [CRC 7.902.] Unless an exception applies, such beneficiaries must either receive or waive notice. (4) The petition does not address venue. Please provide a courtesy copy of the petition and a supplement to the petition, providing the missing documents, to the assigned examiner by 8/27/25. Without the trust and other documents, the Court cannot determine who is entitled to notice. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. NB: Except for exhibits, each paper filed with the court must bear a footer in the bottom margin of each page, placed below the page number and divided from the rest of the document page by a printed line. [CRC 2.110(a).]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF RONALDO ORDONEZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing Status of closing the estate will be set for 7/21/26. The administrator must file either a petition for final distribution or a petition for approval of report of status of administration pursuant to Prob. C. Section 12200 (requesting additional time for estate administration) at least two weeks before 7/21/26. Appearance will be required on that status date if no petition has been filed. NB: A Judicial Council form must not be used to supplement a pleading. [CRC 7.104(b)(2).] The local form, Declaration of Real Property, has not been required for many years.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JOHN ELWIN CARMAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MARY A.L. CHUNG
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 8/20/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Item 3g(1)(d) of the petition should have been completed. (2) Item 8 does not list all persons mentioned in the will. (3) Alternate executors must receive notice. [Probate Code Section 8110(b).] (4) If the petitioner is also the trustee of the devisee trust, all trust beneficiaries must be listed and clearly identified as such in item 8 of the petition. Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 8/6/25. (A supplement, printed on pleading paper and signed and verified by the petitioner, should suffice to provide the information that is missing in the petition.) Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.
